title: chromie.dev - the fastest way to build automations
excerpt: The chromie browser extension pairs live page context with streaming AI codegen: planning tied to the live page, selectable implementation skills, and built-in testing, from Puppeteer runs to BrowserUse-style agents.

body.text: Most "page automation" workflows still mean writing brittle selectors by hand, reloading extensions until something sticks, and guessing whether the live page structure matches what you imagined. **chromie.dev** is built to shorten that loop, starting from the **chromie browser extension**, which captures real tab context and feeds it into the same codegen stack we ship on **chromie**.
Here is how we think about speed, awareness of the document, skills, and testing, and how those pieces show up in the product today.

body.alt: chromie.dev sidebar on Amazon with Amazon Price Highlighter code editor and yellow price highlights on search results
body.caption: The chromie extension sidebar on a live Amazon page: edit, test, and save, while prices are highlighted on the real page.

body.text: Speed: describe it once, generate runnable code

The core loop is conversational: you describe what you want on the page, and the backend streams generated JavaScript userscripts (vanilla JS, wrapped for safe injection), following strict output rules (summary + ```javascript``` block + JSON metadata for match patterns and run timing). That streaming path is tuned so you are not waiting for a monolithic blob before you see progress.

Because generation is incremental and the UI updates as code arrives, iteration feels closer to "pair programming" than "submit a ticket and hope." The chromie extension keeps you anchored on the site you care about while the model produces the script.
Sharp awareness of the page, not guessing selectors in the dark

Blind codegen is how automations break: the model invents selectors that never existed. chromie addresses this with a **planning stage**: we pass a semantic outline of the page (what regions matter, which attributes appear in the sketch, timing hints) into the system prompt as a structured briefing, not a dump of the entire tree, but enough to anchor selectors to reality.

The coding agent is instructed to treat that briefing as primary context: prefer **primary** selectors from the plan, always pair them with **fallback** queries, null-check results, and follow an explicit timing contract: static trees versus MutationObserver versus bounded polling, so dynamic single-page apps do not silently win.

When something cannot be verified from the outline, the pipeline marks **inferred** paths and warns instead of pretending certainty. That discipline is what we mean by sharp awareness: fewer fantasy selectors, more grounded behavior on messy production pages.
Skills: reusable implementation patterns per request

Not every automation needs the same toolkit. chromie maintains a **skills catalog** (resilience against layout shifts, UI mounting, extraction patterns, and similar chunks). A selector step picks only the skills that fit the user request, then injects that context into the system prompt.
Effectively, each run gets a mini playbook: scoped guidance instead of one giant generic prompt, so the model spends tokens on what matters for *this* site and *this* task.
Testing features that match how extensions actually run

Fast codegen is not enough if you cannot prove the script works. chromie wires generated userscripts into a **test harness** that mirrors Manifest V3 content-script execution (wrapped IIFE, match patterns, run_at) so you exercise the same code path users get.

On the full builder side, we generate **Puppeteer**-style tests aligned with Chrome's official extension-testing guidance: waiting for the service worker, opening popups and options pages through supported APIs, and driving real browser targets instead of hand-waved "click the icon" steps.

For richer flows, we also produce **BrowserUse-style** agent scripts: executable scenarios that navigate extension URLs via placeholders (popup, options, side panel) and separate "switch pages" from "interact on the current page", reducing flaky tests that re-navigate or repeat the same URL for no reason.
Why we call it the fastest path

Speed comes from the combination: live page context from the extension, streaming codegen, planning grounded in the real document, skill-scoped prompts, and tests you can run before you ship. If you are tired of selector roulette and manual reload loops, this stack is what we built chromie for.
Install the **chromie extension** from the Chrome Web Store to run automations on real sites, or start from **chromie.dev** when you want the full builder and testing workflow.

title: How Extensions Can Supercharge AI Agents
excerpt: AI agents on the web still struggle with token efficiency, memory, and security. Chrome extensions operate in the right layer to provide deterministic automation, persistent workflows, and practical guardrails.

body.text: AI agents are improving quickly, but anyone using them seriously on the open web runs into the same reality: **the browser was built for humans, not autonomous systems**.

Today’s agents can reason, click buttons, fill forms, and navigate websites. But once tasks become longer-horizon or repetitive, they hit predictable bottlenecks. In my view, three matter most: tokens, memory, and security.
This is where Chrome extensions become unexpectedly powerful.

Extensions already sit inside the browser layer. They can read pages, modify interfaces, automate workflows, and persist state across sessions. Historically, they’ve been built for consumers: ad blockers, grammar tools, coupon finders, productivity hacks. But there’s a new opportunity emerging—extensions built for agents.
Why Agents Struggle on the Web

Unlike code generation, where mistakes happen in a relatively sandboxed environment, browser actions often have real-world consequences. Misclicking “Submit Payment” is different from writing buggy Python.
As I see it, agents are constrained in three ways:
1. Tokens: Agents often spend expensive tokens performing simple, reproducible tasks.
These are low-value actions that don’t require fresh reasoning every time, yet many agents re-solve them from scratch on each run.
2. Memory: Even capable agents frequently forget useful workflows.
They solve a complex sequence once, then encounter the same problem later and burn tokens rediscovering the solution. This becomes more painful as tasks stretch across days, accounts, and websites.
3. Security: The modern web is noisy and adversarial.

Prompt injections hidden in pages, deceptive UI, sponsored buttons designed to mimic primary actions, accidental purchases, malicious redirects. Humans already struggle with this environment. Agents will too.
Why Extensions Solve This Cleanly
Chrome extensions operate at the right abstraction layer: directly inside the browser.
Instead of forcing every model to reason through raw websites, extensions can shape the environment before the agent interacts with it.
Automation
Extensions can handle repetitive browser actions deterministically.
Auto-fill forms, click known flows, navigate standard menus, extract recurring data patterns. This reduces token usage and speeds execution.
Long-Term Memory
Extensions can store useful knowledge locally in-browser.
Think of it as a persistent skills.md for agents: known workflows, prior successful actions, preferred selectors, account-specific logic, reusable routines.
Instead of needing a vector database for every lightweight use case, some memory can simply live where the work happens.
Security Filters
Extensions can remove or hide risky elements before the model sees them.
Ads, fake CTAs, prompt injection text, suspicious overlays, irrelevant clutter. They can also gate irreversible actions behind confirmations.
This matters more as agents become trusted with purchasing, operations, and internal workflows.
Better Perception
Most agents use some mix of visual understanding and HTML parsing. Extensions can improve both.

They can annotate DOM elements with structured attributes, label important controls, simplify layouts, or visually highlight intended paths. In effect, they can make websites easier for agents to understand than they are today.
Why This Market Is Bigger Than Consumer Extensions
Consumer extensions are popular, but crowded. Distribution is noisy, retention is hard, and many categories are already owned by incumbents.
Agent infrastructure is earlier.

Every company experimenting with AI assistants, internal copilots, browser automation, or customer workflow agents will eventually run into browser bottlenecks. Extensions are one of the most practical ways to solve them without rebuilding the web.
That makes this especially interesting for enterprises.
Where chromie.dev Fits
This is part of why I’m building chromie.dev.
If extensions become a core primitive for agents, they need to be faster to build, test, maintain, and distribute.
That means:

items: Building Chrome extensions from natural language prompts
Testing them from an agent’s perspective
Continuously monitoring breakage when selectors or websites change
Creating a marketplace for extensions purpose-built for agents, not humans

body.text: The Chrome Web Store wasn’t designed for this future. It was designed for consumers.
Final Thought
For years, extensions were treated as browser accessories.
I think they may become infrastructure.
As agents move from short demos to long-horizon real work, they’ll need memory, guardrails, efficiency, and cleaner environments. Chrome extensions can provide all four—today, with existing tooling.
The browser may still be built for humans. But extensions might be how we adapt it for agents.

title: How chromie Secured Its First Customer: OmniSpeech
excerpt: After customer interviews revealed mixed interest, a chance meeting at a University of Maryland startup conference led to chromie.dev's first customer—OmniSpeech, an AI deepfake detection platform.

body.text: Finding our first customer

After building the initial prototype for chromie.dev, we were eager to pitch the product we had so excitedly built. However, after conducting several customer interviews, we realized our product wasn't as unanimously exciting as we had thought. We remained bullish on chromie.dev, but understood that some companies didn't have a real need for our product. Until we met OmniSpeech.

I ran into David at a start-up conference at the University of Maryland, where he initially pitched me his product, OmniSpeech, an AI deepfake detection platform. Curious, I asked how he monetized the powerful technology, to which he mentioned their Zoom AI Detection. Almost instinctively, I asked if they had brought the product to Chrome. They hadn't, and now it was my turn to pitch! I explained chromie.dev's value proposition to David, explaining that their sole focus will/should remain building their Deepfake Technology and chromie.dev will bridge their gap to a Chrome Extension. To my surprise, he agreed! chromie.dev finally secured its first customer, and identified a new segment of product-customer fit.
From handshake to contract

After some demos and contract negotiations, we finally signed our contract with OmniSpeech! Our prototype was ready the same day, and in the following weeks, we met with OmniSpeech to integrate their API updates into the extension.
Beta testing and launch

Leveraging chromie's secure sharing, we easily added beta testers to the extension without directly publishing to the Chrome Web Store. After some minor bugs and styling issues were complete, we were ready to go to market. Using our novel Deployment Wizard, we swiftly met Chrome Web Store publishing requirements and posted their now live extension!

title: Testing Features for Chrome Extensions
excerpt: Manually testing Chrome extensions is tedious. Click through your extension, check if it works, make a change, repeat. We got tired of it, so we built something better: automated tests that actually understand what your extension does.
content: Manually testing Chrome extensions is tedious. Click through your extension, check if it works, make a change, repeat. We got tired of it, so we built something better: automated tests that actually understand what your extension does.

title: Why we built chromie
excerpt: The founder of Promptly AI (20K users, acquired) shares how extension development frustrations led to building chromie.dev—an AI-centered ecosystem designed for extension developers.

body.text: From Promptly to chromie
For the past year, I've dedicated myself to building and growing Promptly AI, a prompt engineering Chrome extension with 20,000 users that was recently acquired by another startup.

My journey with Promptly began simply: I recognized the immense power of AI chatbots, but also the frustration of prompt engineering. Getting the best results was often tedious, and many users didn't know where to start. My goal was to solve a problem I personally experienced by creating a very simple Chrome extension.

Promptly's core feature was a one-click prompt optimizer. It injected a button into services like ChatGPT, extracted the user's current prompt, and replaced it with an optimized, "engineered" version. The technology under the hood was straightforward: an LLM tasked with refining input to another LLM—it was AI for AI.
Battling development friction
However, while the core product concept was simple, the process of building and maintaining the extension was anything but. I was constantly battling development friction:

items: Dealing with complex and often confusing Chrome APIs.
The tedious process of scraping and adapting to changing website structures.
The slow, convoluted cycle of having to manually reload the extension after every single code edit.

body.text: This constant struggle with the convoluted Chrome extension development process sparked my next idea. I built chromie.dev because I was my first customer.
Building chromie.dev

I realized the community needed a dedicated, streamlined environment. chromie.dev is not just another AI coding tool; it's a complete ecosystem designed to eliminate the friction I faced. My vision was clear: to create an AI-centered coding tool that integrates essential features for real-world projects—including built-in testing, easy sharing, and painless deployment.

While it's true that any AI tool can help generate an extension, chromie.dev's differentiation is in its focus. It's built by an extension developer for the extension developer. It's designed for the side project you want to take public, solving the exact pain points that nearly derailed Promptly AI.
